title_en: Zakat, Fasting, and Hajj in Al-Mabsut: Advanced Hanafi Analysis
title_ar: الزكاة والصيام والحج في المبسوط: التحليل الحنفي المتعمق
content_en:
As-Sarakhsi's treatment of the three remaining pillars of Islam in Al-Mabsut provides the advanced analytical treatment of these obligations that makes the work the definitive Hanafi reference for complex questions in this area.

For zakat, the most analytically developed sections of Al-Mabsut concern the relationship between the zakat obligation and business partnership structures. When multiple partners jointly own assets that exceed the nisab threshold, how is the zakat obligation allocated? As-Sarakhsi presents the Hanafi school's positions on zakat for joint ownership, for goods held in mudharabah (capital-labor partnership), and for goods whose ownership status is disputed — reflecting the practical complexity of commercial arrangements in the medieval Islamic economy.

The sections on distributing zakat in Al-Mabsut address the Hanafi school's position that distribution to a single category of recipients is permissible — the giver may concentrate all his zakat on the poor (fuqara') without being required to distribute among all eight Quranic categories. As-Sarakhsi presents this permissive position and its basis, noting that the Quran establishes who may receive zakat but does not require equal distribution among all eligible categories.

Fasting in Al-Mabsut receives particularly detailed treatment on the question of what constitutes food and drink for fasting purposes. As-Sarakhsi addresses the Hanafi school's characteristic thoroughness on this question: anything that enters the body cavity (jawf) through an open natural pathway breaks the fast. He discusses the specific applications of this principle — ear drops (break the fast if they reach the brain or throat), nose drops (break the fast if they reach the throat), suppositories (break the fast because they enter through a natural opening into the body cavity). This systematic application of the 'open pathway to body cavity' principle reflects Hanafi legal methodology at its most systematic.

The expiation (kaffara) for deliberate fast-breaking in Ramadan is treated in Al-Mabsut with the Hanafi school's positions on who is required to perform expiation. As-Sarakhsi addresses cases where the obligation to pay kaffara may be reduced or transferred: the person who has sexual intercourse with his wife during Ramadan must pay kaffara; the wife who is forced is not required to pay kaffara for herself; the slave who is forced must pay kaffara but cannot perform the freeing option.

Hajj in Al-Mabsut is addressed with as-Sarakhsi's comprehensive treatment of the rites and their legal classification. His analysis of the muhsar (person prevented from completing hajj) is particularly detailed — the Hanafi school's positions on the conditions for valid prevention, the method of exiting the ihram state, and the obligation to make up the missed hajj are all addressed with attention to the Quranic verse on ihsar (2:196) and the prophetic practice during the Hudaybiyyah episode.
